architecture.summary: REALM augments language model pre-training with a learned neural knowledge retriever that operates over a large text corpus (Wikipedia). The system decomposes prediction into two steps: (1) a dense retriever using BERT-style Transformers with inner-product scoring to retrieve top-k documents from a corpus via Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS), and (2) a knowledge-augmented encoder (separate Transformer) that concatenates the input with retrieved documents and produces predictions. Both components are jointly pre-trained on masked language modeling with unsupervised backpropagation through the retrieval step, then fine-tuned on Open-QA. The retriever embeddings are asynchronously re-indexed every ~500 training steps to keep the MIPS index fresh despite ongoing gradient updates.

claims.statement: REALM outperforms all previous methods by a significant margin on three popular Open-QA benchmarks
On WebQuestions, REALM achieves 40.2% exact match accuracy
On CuratedTrec, REALM achieves 46.8% exact match accuracy
REALM outperforms the largest T5-11B model while being 30 times smaller
Salient span masking (masking named entities and dates rather than random tokens) is crucial for REALM, significantly outperforming random token masking and random span masking schemes
Both the encoder and retriever benefit from REALM pre-training separately, but the best result requires both components acting in unison
A stale MIPS index (30 steps without refresh) significantly hurts model training, reducing exact match from 38.2% to 28.7%
Pre-training was conducted for 200k steps on 64 Google Cloud TPUs with batch size 512
